calendar.matters:
NOTICE OF SECOND PETITION TO ENFORCE SUBPOENA
Off Calendar May-22-2026 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference:
Ryan Bailey, Esq., of BUCHER LAW, PLLC, Tel: (650) 881-2201 for petitioner.
Roger Huddle, Esq., of QUINN EMANUEL URQUHART & SULLIVAN, LLP, Tel: (213) 443-3285 for respondent.

Hearimg Held. A timely Notice of Contest was received from respondent counsel regarding the entirety of the Court's tentative ruling. Respondent's counsel argued that service of the subpoena was improper; however, no such issue or supporting documentation was included in the papers submitted to the Court. The matter argued/discussed. The Court, having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral arguments, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

1 - Petitioner Hugh Phillips' second petition to enforce subpoena is GRANTED.

Section 7 of the Federal Arbitration Act ("FAA"), 9 U.S.C. section 7, provides that "the United States district court" may compel compliance with an arbitrator subpoena. Because the FAA does not itself confer federal question jurisdiction, a party seeking to enforce a subpoena under the FAA must establish a basis for subject matter jurisdiction. (See Maine Community Health Options v. Albertsons Companies, Inc., 993 F.3d 720, 722 (9th Cir. 2021); Moses H. Cone Mem'l Hosp. v. Mercury Constr. Corp., 460 U.S. 1, 25 n.32 (1983) ["The Arbitration Act is something of an anomaly in the field of federal-court jurisdiction. It creates a body of federal substantive law establishing and regulating the duty to honor an agreement to arbitrate, yet it does not create any independent federal-question jurisdiction"]; Stolt-Nielsen, SA v. Celanese AG, 430 F.3d 567, 572 (2d Cir. 2005); Amgen, Inc. v. Kidney Ctr. of Del. Cnty., Ltd., 95 F.3d 562, 567 (7th Cir. 1996).) In this case, there is no basis for federal court jurisdiction and allowing this case to proceed in state court is consistent with the FAA. (See Aixtron, Inc. v. Veeco Instruments Inc. (2020) 52 Cal.App.5th 360 [examining the substantive propriety of an arbitrator's discovery order under the FAA].

Good cause appearing, the subpoena having properly issued and the record demonstrating the requirements of section 7 are met, the court orders enforcement of the arbitrator's subpoena addressed to Wolfire Games, LLC. Wolfire Games, LLC shall appear with the requested documents and testify on the date determined by the arbitrator in AAA Case No. 1-23-0005-3496. (See 12/16/2025 Bailey Decl., Ex. 1.) The fact the original date for compliance has run is of no moment. Wolfire's contention there is no agreement to arbitrate is unavailing. An arbitration is pending and the arbitrator has issued a valid subpoena which the court herein enforces. Wolfire can address its protestations regarding the scope of the document demand to the arbitrator. (See Washington Nat'l Ins. Co. v. OBEX Group LLC (2d Cir. 2020) 958 F.3d 126, 138-139.) In any event, Wolfire failed to show any protective order inhibits (let alone bars) compliance.

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
